4.play: Of semblable import,--but he hath waged

5.play: New wars 'gainst Pompey; made his will, and read it

6.play: To public ear:

7.play: Spoke scantly of me: when perforce he could not

8.play: But pay me terms of honour, cold and sickly

9.play: He vented them; most narrow measure lent me:

10.play: When the best hint was given him, he not took't,

11.play: Or did it from his teeth.

12.play: OCTAVIA: O my good lord,

13.play: Believe not all; or, if you must believe,

14.play: Stomach not all. A more unhappy lady,

15.play: If this division chance, ne'er stood between,

16.play: Praying for both parts:

17.play: The good gods me presently,

18.play: When I shall pray, 'O bless my lord and husband!'

19.play: Undo that prayer, by crying out as loud,

20.play: 'O, bless my brother!' Husband win, win brother,

21.play: Prays, and destroys the prayer; no midway

22.play: 'Twixt these extremes at all.

23.play: MARK ANTONY: Gentle Octavia,

24.play: Let your best love draw to that point, which seeks

25.play: Best to preserve it: if I lose mine honour,

26.play: I lose myself: better I were not yours

27.play: Than yours so branchless. But, as you requested,

28.play: Yourself shall go between 's: the mean time, lady,

29.play: I'll raise the preparation of a war

30.play: Shall stain your brother: make your soonest haste;

31.play: So your desires are yours.

32.play: OCTAVIA: Thanks to my lord.

33.play: The Jove of power make me most weak, most weak,

34.play: Your reconciler! Wars 'twixt you twain would be

35.play: As if the world should cleave, and that slain men

36.play: Should solder up the rift.

37.play: MARK ANTONY: When it appears to you where this begins,

38.play: Turn your displeasure that way: for our faults

39.play: Can never be so equal, that your love

40.play: Can equally move with them. Provide your going;

41.play: Choose your own company, and command what cost

42.play: Your heart has mind to.

1.play: Enter DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS and EROS, meeting

2.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: How now, friend Eros!

3.play: EROS: There's strange news come, sir.

4.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: What, man?

5.play: EROS: Caesar and Lepidus have made wars upon Pompey.

6.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: This is old: what is the success?

7.play: EROS: Caesar, having made use of him in the wars 'gainst

8.play: Pompey, presently denied him rivality; would not let

9.play: him partake in the glory of the action: and not

10.play: resting here, accuses him of letters he had formerly

11.play: wrote to Pompey; upon his own appeal, seizes him: so

12.play: the poor third is up, till death enlarge his confine.

13.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Then, world, thou hast a pair of chaps, no more;

14.play: And throw between them all the food thou hast,

15.play: They'll grind the one the other. Where's Antony?

16.play: EROS: He's walking in the garden--thus; and spurns

17.play: The rush that lies before him; cries, 'Fool Lepidus!'

18.play: And threats the throat of that his officer

19.play: That murder'd Pompey.

20.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Our great navy's rigg'd.

21.play: EROS: For Italy and Caesar. More, Domitius;

22.play: My lord desires you presently: my news

23.play: I might have told hereafter.

24.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: 'Twill be naught:

25.play: But let it be. Bring me to Antony.

26.play: EROS: Come, sir.

1.play: Enter OCTAVIUS CAESAR, AGRIPPA, and MECAENAS

2.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Contemning Rome, he has done all this, and more,

3.play: In Alexandria: here's the manner of 't:

4.play: I' the market-place, on a tribunal silver'd,

5.play: Cleopatra and himself in chairs of gold

6.play: Were publicly enthroned: at the feet sat

7.play: Caesarion, whom they call my father's son,

8.play: And all the unlawful issue that their lust

9.play: Since then hath made between them. Unto her

10.play: He gave the stablishment of Egypt; made her

11.play: Of lower Syria, Cyprus, Lydia,

12.play: Absolute queen.

13.play: MECAENAS: This in the public eye?

14.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: I' the common show-place, where they exercise.

15.play: His sons he there proclaim'd the kings of kings:

16.play: Great Media, Parthia, and Armenia.

17.play: He gave to Alexander; to Ptolemy he assign'd

18.play: Syria, Cilicia, and Phoenicia: she

19.play: In the habiliments of the goddess Isis

20.play: That day appear'd; and oft before gave audience,

21.play: As 'tis reported, so.

22.play: MECAENAS: Let Rome be thus Inform'd.

23.play: AGRIPPA: Who, queasy with his insolence

24.play: Already, will their good thoughts call from him.

25.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: The people know it; and have now received

26.play: His accusations.

27.play: AGRIPPA: Who does he accuse?

28.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Caesar: and that, having in Sicily

29.play: Sextus Pompeius spoil'd, we had not rated him

30.play: His part o' the isle: then does he say, he lent me

31.play: Some shipping unrestored: lastly, he frets

32.play: That Lepidus of the triumvirate

33.play: Should be deposed; and, being, that we detain

34.play: All his revenue.

35.play: AGRIPPA: Sir, this should be answer'd.

36.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: 'Tis done already, and the messenger gone.

37.play: I have told him, Lepidus was grown too cruel;

38.play: That he his high authority abused,

39.play: And did deserve his change: for what I have conquer'd,

40.play: I grant him part; but then, in his Armenia,

41.play: And other of his conquer'd kingdoms, I

42.play: Demand the like.

43.play: MECAENAS: He'll never yield to that.

44.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Nor must not then be yielded to in this.

45.play: OCTAVIA: Hail, Caesar, and my lord! hail, most dear Caesar!

46.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: That ever I should call thee castaway!

47.play: OCTAVIA: You have not call'd me so, nor have you cause.

48.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Why have you stol'n upon us thus! You come not

49.play: Like Caesar's sister: the wife of Antony

50.play: Should have an army for an usher, and

51.play: The neighs of horse to tell of her approach

52.play: Long ere she did appear; the trees by the way

53.play: Should have borne men; and expectation fainted,

54.play: Longing for what it had not; nay, the dust

55.play: Should have ascended to the roof of heaven,

56.play: Raised by your populous troops: but you are come

57.play: A market-maid to Rome; and have prevented

58.play: The ostentation of our love, which, left unshown,

59.play: Is often left unloved; we should have met you

60.play: By sea and land; supplying every stage

63.play: To come thus was I not constrain'd, but did

64.play: On my free will. My lord, Mark Antony,

65.play: Hearing that you prepared for war, acquainted

66.play: My grieved ear withal; whereon, I begg'd

68.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Which soon he granted,

69.play: Being an obstruct 'tween his lust and him.

70.play: OCTAVIA: Do not say so, my lord.

71.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: I have eyes upon him,

72.play: And his affairs come to me on the wind.

75.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: No, my most wronged sister; Cleopatra

76.play: Hath nodded him to her. He hath given his empire

77.play: Up to a whore; who now are levying

78.play: The kings o' the earth for war; he hath assembled

79.play: Bocchus, the king of Libya; Archelaus,

81.play: Of Paphlagonia; the Thracian king, Adallas;

82.play: King Malchus of Arabia; King of Pont;

85.play: The kings of Mede and Lycaonia,

86.play: With a more larger list of sceptres.

88.play: That have my heart parted betwixt two friends

91.play: Your letters did withhold our breaking forth;

92.play: Till we perceived, both how you were wrong led,

93.play: And we in negligent danger. Cheer your heart;

94.play: Be you not troubled with the time, which drives

95.play: O'er your content these strong necessities;

96.play: But let determined things to destiny

97.play: Hold unbewail'd their way. Welcome to Rome;

98.play: Nothing more dear to me. You are abused

99.play: Beyond the mark of thought: and the high gods,

100.play: To do you justice, make them ministers

101.play: Of us and those that love you. Best of comfort;

105.play: Each heart in Rome does love and pity you:

106.play: Only the adulterous Antony, most large

107.play: In his abominations, turns you off;

108.play: And gives his potent regiment to a trull,

111.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Most certain. Sister, welcome: pray you,

112.play: Be ever known to patience: my dear'st sister!

1.play: Enter CLEOPATRA and DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS

2.play: CLEOPATRA: I will be even with thee, doubt it not.

3.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: But why, why, why?

4.play: CLEOPATRA: Thou hast forspoke my being in these wars,

5.play: And say'st it is not fit.

6.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Well, is it, is it?

7.play: CLEOPATRA: If not denounced against us, why should not we

8.play: Be there in person?

9.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: [Aside]  Well, I could reply:

10.play: If we should serve with horse and mares together,

11.play: The horse were merely lost; the mares would bear

12.play: A soldier and his horse.

13.play: CLEOPATRA: What is't you say?

14.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Your presence needs must puzzle Antony;

15.play: Take from his heart, take from his brain,

16.play: from's time,

17.play: What should not then be spared. He is already

18.play: Traduced for levity; and 'tis said in Rome

19.play: That Photinus an eunuch and your maids

20.play: Manage this war.

21.play: CLEOPATRA: Sink Rome, and their tongues rot

22.play: That speak against us! A charge we bear i' the war,

23.play: And, as the president of my kingdom, will

24.play: Appear there for a man. Speak not against it:

25.play: I will not stay behind.

26.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Nay, I have done.

27.play: Here comes the emperor.

28.play: MARK ANTONY: Is it not strange, Canidius,

29.play: That from Tarentum and Brundusium

30.play: He could so quickly cut the Ionian sea,

31.play: And take in Toryne? You have heard on't, sweet?

32.play: CLEOPATRA: Celerity is never more admired

33.play: Than by the negligent.

34.play: MARK ANTONY: A good rebuke,

35.play: Which might have well becomed the best of men,

36.play: To taunt at slackness. Canidius, we

37.play: Will fight with him by sea.

38.play: CLEOPATRA: By sea! what else?

39.play: CANIDIUS: Why will my lord do so?

40.play: MARK ANTONY: For that he dares us to't.

41.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: So hath my lord dared him to single fight.

42.play: CANIDIUS: Ay, and to wage this battle at Pharsalia.

43.play: Where Caesar fought with Pompey: but these offers,

44.play: Which serve not for his vantage, be shakes off;

45.play: And so should you.

46.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Your ships are not well mann'd;

47.play: Your mariners are muleters, reapers, people

48.play: Ingross'd by swift impress; in Caesar's fleet

49.play: Are those that often have 'gainst Pompey fought:

50.play: Their ships are yare; yours, heavy: no disgrace

51.play: Shall fall you for refusing him at sea,

52.play: Being prepared for land.

53.play: MARK ANTONY: By sea, by sea.

54.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Most worthy sir, you therein throw away

55.play: The absolute soldiership you have by land;

56.play: Distract your army, which doth most consist

57.play: Of war-mark'd footmen; leave unexecuted

58.play: Your own renowned knowledge; quite forego

59.play: The way which promises assurance; and

60.play: Give up yourself merely to chance and hazard,

62.play: MARK ANTONY: I'll fight at sea.

63.play: CLEOPATRA: I have sixty sails, Caesar none better.

64.play: MARK ANTONY: Our overplus of shipping will we burn;

65.play: And, with the rest full-mann'd, from the head of Actium

66.play: Beat the approaching Caesar. But if we fail,

67.play: We then can do't at land.

69.play: Can he be there in person? 'tis impossible;

70.play: Strange that power should be. Canidius,

71.play: Our nineteen legions thou shalt hold by land,

72.play: And our twelve thousand horse. We'll to our ship:

76.play: CANIDIUS: Soldier, thou art: but his whole action grows

77.play: Not in the power on't: so our leader's led,

80.play: Publicola, and Caelius, are for sea:

81.play: But we keep whole by land. This speed of Caesar's

85.play: With news the time's with labour, and throes forth,

1.play: Enter OCTAVIUS CAESAR, and TAURUS, with his army, marching

2.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Taurus!

3.play: TAURUS: My lord?

4.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Strike not by land; keep whole: provoke not battle,

5.play: Till we have done at sea. Do not exceed

6.play: The prescript of this scroll: our fortune lies

7.play: Upon this jump.

1.play: Enter MARK ANTONY and DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS

2.play: MARK ANTONY: Set we our squadrons on yond side o' the hill,

3.play: In eye of Caesar's battle; from which place

4.play: We may the number of the ships behold,

5.play: And so proceed accordingly.

1.play: Alarum. Enter DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS

2.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Naught, naught all, naught! I can behold no longer:

3.play: The Antoniad, the Egyptian admiral,

4.play: With all their sixty, fly and turn the rudder:

5.play: To see't mine eyes are blasted.

6.play: SCARUS: Gods and goddesses,

7.play: All the whole synod of them!

8.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: What's thy passion!

9.play: SCARUS: The greater cantle of the world is lost

10.play: With very ignorance; we have kiss'd away

11.play: Kingdoms and provinces.

12.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: How appears the fight?

13.play: SCARUS: On our side like the token'd pestilence,

14.play: Where death is sure. Yon ribaudred nag of Egypt,--

15.play: Whom leprosy o'ertake!--i' the midst o' the fight,

16.play: When vantage like a pair of twins appear'd,

17.play: Both as the same, or rather ours the elder,

18.play: The breese upon her, like a cow in June,

19.play: Hoists sails and flies.

20.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: That I beheld:

21.play: Mine eyes did sicken at the sight, and could not

22.play: Endure a further view.

23.play: SCARUS: She once being loof'd,

24.play: The noble ruin of her magic, Antony,

25.play: Claps on his sea-wing, and, like a doting mallard,

26.play: Leaving the fight in height, flies after her:

27.play: I never saw an action of such shame;

28.play: Experience, manhood, honour, ne'er before

29.play: Did violate so itself.

30.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Alack, alack!

31.play: CANIDIUS: Our fortune on the sea is out of breath,

32.play: And sinks most lamentably. Had our general

33.play: Been what he knew himself, it had gone well:

34.play: O, he has given example for our flight,

35.play: Most grossly, by his own!

36.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Ay, are you thereabouts?

37.play: Why, then, good night indeed.

38.play: CANIDIUS: Toward Peloponnesus are they fled.

39.play: SCARUS: 'Tis easy to't; and there I will attend

40.play: What further comes.

41.play: CANIDIUS: To Caesar will I render

42.play: My legions and my horse: six kings already

43.play: Show me the way of yielding.

44.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: I'll yet follow

45.play: The wounded chance of Antony, though my reason

46.play: Sits in the wind against me.

1.play: Enter MARK ANTONY with Attendants

2.play: MARK ANTONY: Hark! the land bids me tread no more upon't;

3.play: It is ashamed to bear me! Friends, come hither:

4.play: I am so lated in the world, that I

5.play: Have lost my way for ever: I have a ship

6.play: Laden with gold; take that, divide it; fly,

7.play: And make your peace with Caesar.

8.play: I have fled myself; and have instructed cowards

9.play: To run and show their shoulders. Friends, be gone;

10.play: I have myself resolved upon a course

11.play: Which has no need of you; be gone:

12.play: My treasure's in the harbour, take it. O,

13.play: I follow'd that I blush to look upon:

14.play: My very hairs do mutiny; for the white

15.play: Reprove the brown for rashness, and they them

16.play: For fear and doting. Friends, be gone: you shall

17.play: Have letters from me to some friends that will

18.play: Sweep your way for you. Pray you, look not sad,

19.play: Nor make replies of loathness: take the hint

20.play: Which my despair proclaims; let that be left

21.play: Which leaves itself: to the sea-side straightway:

22.play: I will possess you of that ship and treasure.

23.play: Leave me, I pray, a little: pray you now:

24.play: Nay, do so; for, indeed, I have lost command,

25.play: Therefore I pray you: I'll see you by and by.

26.play: EROS: Nay, gentle madam, to him, comfort him.

27.play: IRAS: Do, most dear queen.

28.play: CHARMIAN: Do! why: what else?

29.play: CLEOPATRA: Let me sit down. O Juno!

30.play: MARK ANTONY: No, no, no, no, no.

31.play: EROS: See you here, sir?

32.play: MARK ANTONY: O fie, fie, fie!

33.play: CHARMIAN: Madam!

34.play: IRAS: Madam, O good empress!

35.play: EROS: Sir, sir,--

36.play: MARK ANTONY: Yes, my lord, yes; he at Philippi kept

37.play: His sword e'en like a dancer; while I struck

38.play: The lean and wrinkled Cassius; and 'twas I

39.play: That the mad Brutus ended: he alone

40.play: Dealt on lieutenantry, and no practise had

41.play: In the brave squares of war: yet now--No matter.

42.play: CLEOPATRA: Ah, stand by.

43.play: EROS: The queen, my lord, the queen.

44.play: IRAS: Go to him, madam, speak to him:

45.play: He is unqualitied with very shame.

46.play: CLEOPATRA: Well then, sustain him: O!

47.play: EROS: Most noble sir, arise; the queen approaches:

48.play: Her head's declined, and death will seize her, but

49.play: Your comfort makes the rescue.

50.play: MARK ANTONY: I have offended reputation,

51.play: A most unnoble swerving.

52.play: EROS: Sir, the queen.

53.play: MARK ANTONY: O, whither hast thou led me, Egypt? See,

54.play: How I convey my shame out of thine eyes

55.play: By looking back what I have left behind

56.play: 'Stroy'd in dishonour.

57.play: CLEOPATRA: O my lord, my lord,

58.play: Forgive my fearful sails! I little thought

60.play: MARK ANTONY: Egypt, thou knew'st too well

61.play: My heart was to thy rudder tied by the strings,

62.play: And thou shouldst tow me after: o'er my spirit

63.play: Thy full supremacy thou knew'st, and that

64.play: Thy beck might from the bidding of the gods

68.play: To the young man send humble treaties, dodge

69.play: And palter in the shifts of lowness; who

70.play: With half the bulk o' the world play'd as I pleased,

71.play: Making and marring fortunes. You did know

72.play: How much you were my conqueror; and that

73.play: My sword, made weak by my affection, would

76.play: MARK ANTONY: Fall not a tear, I say; one of them rates

77.play: All that is won and lost: give me a kiss;

78.play: Even this repays me. We sent our schoolmaster;

79.play: Is he come back? Love, I am full of lead.

80.play: Some wine, within there, and our viands! Fortune knows

81.play: We scorn her most when most she offers blows.

1.play: Enter OCTAVIUS CAESAR, DOLABELLA, THYREUS, with others

2.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Let him appear that's come from Antony.

3.play: Know you him?

4.play: DOLABELLA: Caesar, 'tis his schoolmaster:

5.play: An argument that he is pluck'd, when hither

6.play: He sends so poor a pinion off his wing,

7.play: Which had superfluous kings for messengers

8.play: Not many moons gone by.

9.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Approach, and speak.

10.play: EUPHRONIUS: Such as I am, I come from Antony:

11.play: I was of late as petty to his ends

12.play: As is the morn-dew on the myrtle-leaf

13.play: To his grand sea.

14.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Be't so: declare thine office.

15.play: EUPHRONIUS: Lord of his fortunes he salutes thee, and

16.play: Requires to live in Egypt: which not granted,

17.play: He lessens his requests; and to thee sues

18.play: To let him breathe between the heavens and earth,

19.play: A private man in Athens: this for him.

20.play: Next, Cleopatra does confess thy greatness;

21.play: Submits her to thy might; and of thee craves

22.play: The circle of the Ptolemies for her heirs,

23.play: Now hazarded to thy grace.

24.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: For Antony,

25.play: I have no ears to his request. The queen

26.play: Of audience nor desire shall fail, so she

27.play: From Egypt drive her all-disgraced friend,

28.play: Or take his life there: this if she perform,

29.play: She shall not sue unheard. So to them both.

30.play: EUPHRONIUS: Fortune pursue thee!

31.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Bring him through the bands.

32.play: From Antony win Cleopatra: promise,

33.play: And in our name, what she requires; add more,

34.play: From thine invention, offers: women are not

35.play: In their best fortunes strong; but want will perjure

36.play: The ne'er touch'd vestal: try thy cunning, Thyreus;

37.play: Make thine own edict for thy pains, which we

38.play: Will answer as a law.

39.play: THYREUS: Caesar, I go.

40.play: OCTAVIUS CAESAR: Observe how Antony becomes his flaw,

41.play: And what thou think'st his very action speaks

42.play: In every power that moves.

43.play: THYREUS: Caesar, I shall.

1.play: Enter CLEOPATRA, DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS, CHARMIAN, and IRAS

2.play: CLEOPATRA: What shall we do, Enobarbus?

3.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Think, and die.

4.play: CLEOPATRA: Is Antony or we in fault for this?

5.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: Antony only, that would make his will

6.play: Lord of his reason. What though you fled

7.play: From that great face of war, whose several ranges

8.play: Frighted each other? why should he follow?

9.play: The itch of his affection should not then

10.play: Have nick'd his captainship; at such a point,

11.play: When half to half the world opposed, he being

12.play: The meered question: 'twas a shame no less

13.play: Than was his loss, to course your flying flags,

14.play: And leave his navy gazing.

15.play: CLEOPATRA: Prithee, peace.

16.play: MARK ANTONY: Is that his answer?

17.play: EUPHRONIUS: Ay, my lord.

18.play: MARK ANTONY: The queen shall then have courtesy, so she

19.play: Will yield us up.

20.play: EUPHRONIUS: He says so.

21.play: MARK ANTONY: Let her know't.

22.play: To the boy Caesar send this grizzled head,

23.play: And he will fill thy wishes to the brim

24.play: With principalities.

25.play: CLEOPATRA: That head, my lord?

26.play: MARK ANTONY: To him again: tell him he wears the rose

27.play: Of youth upon him; from which the world should note

28.play: Something particular: his coin, ships, legions,

29.play: May be a coward's; whose ministers would prevail

30.play: Under the service of a child as soon

31.play: As i' the command of Caesar: I dare him therefore

32.play: To lay his gay comparisons apart,

33.play: And answer me declined, sword against sword,

34.play: Ourselves alone. I'll write it: follow me.

35.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: [Aside]  Yes, like enough, high-battled Caesar will

36.play: Unstate his happiness, and be staged to the show,

37.play: Against a sworder! I see men's judgments are

38.play: A parcel of their fortunes; and things outward

39.play: Do draw the inward quality after them,

40.play: To suffer all alike. That he should dream,

41.play: Knowing all measures, the full Caesar will

42.play: Answer his emptiness! Caesar, thou hast subdued

43.play: His judgment too.

44.play: CLEOPATRA: What, no more ceremony? See, my women!

45.play: Against the blown rose may they stop their nose

46.play: That kneel'd unto the buds. Admit him, sir.

47.play: DOMITIUS ENOBARBUS: [Aside]  Mine honesty and I begin to square.

48.play: The loyalty well held to fools does make

49.play: Our faith mere folly: yet he that can endure

50.play: To follow with allegiance a fall'n lord

51.play: Does conquer him that did his master conquer

52.play: And earns a place i' the story.

53.play: CLEOPATRA: Caesar's will?
